plan.contextChunk:
property: conditional on $x_2$, variable $x_3$ is independent of
variable $x_1$. The joint probability of all the variables shown here is
$p(x_1, x_2, x_3, y_1, y_2, y_3) =  \phi_{12}(x_1, x_2)  \phi_{23}(x_2, x_3)
\psi_{1}(y_1, x_1) \psi_{2}(y_2, x_2) \psi_{3}(y_3, x_3)$. Using
$p(a,b) = p(a \bigm | b) p(b)$, we can also write the probability of the
$x$ variables conditioned on the observations $y$,
$$p(x_1, x_2, x_3 \bigm | y_1, y_2, y_3) =  
\frac{1}{p(\mathbf{y})} \phi_{12}(x_1, x_2)  \phi_{23}(x_2, x_3) \psi_{1}(y_1, x_1) \psi_{2}(y_2, x_2) \psi_{3}(y_3, x_3).$$
For brevity, we write $p(\mathbf{y})$ for $p(y_1, y_2, y_3)$. Thus, to
form the conditional distribution, within a normalization factor, we
simply include the observed variable values into the joint probability.
For vision applications, we often use such Markov chain structures to
describe events over time.

To capture relationships over space, a two-dimensional (2D) structure is
useful, called a **Markov random field** (MRF) @Blake2011:

![Two-dimensional Markov random field.](figures/graphical_models/mrf.png){width="48%" #fig-leafs}

Then the joint probability over all the variables factorizes into this
product: 

$$p(\mathbf{x} \bigm | \mathbf{y}) = 
\frac{1}{p(\mathbf{y})}
\prod_{(i,j)} \phi_{ij}(x_i, x_j) \prod_i \psi_i(x_i, y_i),$$
where the first product is over all spatial neighbors $i$ and $j$, and the second
product is over all nodes $i$.

An example of how Markov random field models are applied to images is
depicted in @fig-leafs, which show a small image region and its context
in the image (@fig-leafs [a]). @fig-leafs [b] shows an MRF with each
node corresponding to a pixel of the image region. The states of an MRF
can be indicator variables of image segment membership for each pixel.
The states of a hypothetical most-probable configuration of this MRF are
shown as the color of each node in this illustration.

:::{#fig-leafs layout-ncol="2"}
![](figures/graphical_models/leaf2.jpg){width=97% #fig-leafs-a}

![](figures/graphical_models/leaf1.jpg){width=100% #fig-leafs-b}

Fig (a) Image to be segmented and a local region of (a) marked in red.  (b) Visualization of an MRF of nodes corresponding to image pixels inside the marked region, with states indicating segment membership, for a hypothetical most probable configuration.
::: 

## Directed Graphical Models

In addition to undirected graphical models, another type of graphical
model is commonly used. **Directed graphical models** @Koller2009
describe factorizations of the joint probability into products of
conditional probability distributions. Each node in a directed graph
contributes a well-specified factor in the joint probability: the
probability of its variable, conditioned on all the variables originating
arrows pointing into it. So this graph

![A directed graphical model with three variables.](figures/graphical_models/directed.png){width="20%" #eq-directedjoint}

denotes the joint probability,

elements: 9 x-nodes (latent variables, represented as white spheres on a 3x3 grid in the XZ plane)
9 y-nodes (observed variables, represented as grey/colored spheres floating vertically above each x-node)
9 vertical line segments connecting each x-node to its corresponding y-node (representing unary potentials/data terms)
12 horizontal/depth line segments connecting adjacent x-nodes in the 3x3 grid (representing pairwise/spatial potentials)
6 border-extension line segments projecting outward from the edges of the 3x3 grid to indicate an infinite/larger field
Text labels for back-row nodes: 'x1', 'x2', 'x3' and 'y1', 'y2', 'y3'
A semi-transparent ground plane to define the grid's spatial context
Highlight rings around nodes to indicate selection or active Markov blankets

interactions.label: Select Node
interactions.teaches: Clicking a node highlights its Markov blanket: its conditional probability depends only on its spatial neighbors and its vertical observation node.

interactions.label: View Mode
interactions.teaches: Switches between showing the abstract probabilistic graphical model and a physical image segmentation scenario where nodes represent pixel states.
Controls the strength of the spatial pairwise connections; higher values force neighboring pixels to share the same segment label, clearing up noise.

demo_steps.title: The MRF Structure
demo_steps.narration: This is a 2D Markov Random Field (MRF). The white nodes (x) represent hidden true states, while the grey nodes (y) represent noisy observations connected vertically to each hidden state. This structure model spatial relationships across images.

demo_steps.title: Local Independence & Blankets
demo_steps.narration: Click on the central node x2. Notice how it is isolated from the rest of the graph if we condition on its immediate neighbors (the highlighted Markov blanket). In a 2D MRF, a pixel's state depends only on its direct spatial neighbors and its own observation.

demo_steps.title: Noisy Leaf Observations
demo_steps.narration: Switch to Segmentation State. The observed nodes (y) now represent noisy green (leaf) or brown (background) pixels. Without spatial smoothing (smoothness = 0), the estimated hidden states (x) simply mirror this noisy input, resulting in a speckled, low-quality segmentation.

demo_steps.title: Spatial Consistency
demo_steps.narration: By increasing the spatial smoothness parameter, the pairwise connections force neighboring nodes to align. The MRF filters out the noise, producing a clean, coherent boundary between the green leaf and the brown background.

interactionPlan.camera_suggestion: An elevated 3D perspective looking down at the grid, showing the vertical separation between layers clearly.
interactionPlan.view_reasoning: (1) In the original drawing, the horizontal grid lines run left-to-right, and the receding depth lines run diagonally down-left (oblique view). (2) This receding direction implies a camera positioned to the right of the grid looking leftward (negative azimuth). (3) The vertical stalks connecting x and y nodes are fully visible and parallel, suggesting an elevation angle of 20-30 degrees to balance depth perception of the ground grid with vertical stacking clarity.

camera_view.view_notes: Perfect match for the oblique hand-drawn projection; keeps the grid's rows separate and highlights the vertical 2-layer structure.

inline_constraints.default_view: Must align with the 3x3 layout of the provided figure, keeping the y-nodes directly above the x-nodes with identical orientation.
inline_constraints.visible_ui: A single toggle for 'View Mode' and a slider for 'Smoothness' when in segmentation mode, positioned in the top-right corner.
inline_constraints.explainers: Clicking any node displays a small tooltip describing its conditional probability factorization term.
inline_constraints.framing: Ensure the bottom-left and top-right edge-projecting lines are visible, mimicking the infinite-grid hints of the original.
inline_constraints.camera: Match the relative height of the y-stems to the grid spacing so the vertical relationships feel natural and uncluttered.

interactionPlan.notes: In 'Segmentation State', color the y-nodes with a noisy mixture of green and brown (e.g., 6 green, 3 brown). Dynamically solve a tiny 3x3 energy minimization (or simple thresholded smoothing) to color the x-nodes based on the smoothness slider value. If smoothness is low, x-nodes match their noisy y-nodes; as smoothness increases, the x-nodes converge to a clean, segmented 3x3 block (e.g., all left columns green, all right columns brown).

attempts.html: ⚠ Figure failed to load

evaluation.viewing_angle_source: The source figure shows a 3D-like schematic of a Markov Random Field grid. White nodes are positioned in a 3x3 grid on a slightly tilted horizontal plane, and gray nodes are placed vertically above them. The view is from a low-elevation front-left angle, looking towards the back-right.
evaluation.viewing_angle_rendered: The rendered screenshot shows the grid structure from a slightly higher elevation, looking down at the 3x3 grid from the front-left, with a clean orthographic projection.
evaluation.viewing_angle_comparison: The viewing angles are very similar, but the rendered version has a slightly higher camera elevation (looking further down onto the grid) and a more sparse grid layout compared to the tightly packed appearance of the original.
discrepancies: A semi-transparent pale blue ground plane is present in the rendered version, which is absent in the pure-white background of the source figure.
The grid lines extending beyond the 3x3 boundary extend in multiple directions in the render, whereas the source only shows extensions towards the bottom-left and right sides.
The label 'y1' is slightly obscured behind the front-most y-node due to the camera angle alignment.
The nodes in the source are tightly packed horizontally, whereas the rendered version is more spaced out.
evaluation.notes: Excellent pedagogical reproduction of a 2D MRF model with real interactive segmentation solving, with only minor aesthetic differences from the textbook source.
action_items: Remove or hide the pale blue ground plane mesh (groundMat) to match the minimalist white background of the original.
Slightly adjust the camera's azimuth and elevation to prevent node labels (especially y1) from overlapping with nearby foreground nodes.
Reduce the horizontal spacing of the grid nodes to match the more compact aspect ratio of the original figure.
Adjust the border extensions to only project along the bottom-left and right axes as shown in the source.

feedback.rationale: The scores are exceptionally high with an overall average of 4.4, zero failure modes, and excellent interactive functionality, making further aesthetic refinements unnecessary.
action_items: Remove or hide the pale blue ground plane mesh (groundMat) to match the minimalist white background of the original.
Slightly adjust the camera's azimuth and elevation to prevent node labels (especially y1) from overlapping with nearby foreground nodes.
Reduce the horizontal spacing of the grid nodes to match the more compact aspect ratio of the original figure.
Adjust the border extensions to only project along the bottom-left and right axes as shown in the source.
